explanation_md: `noexcept(expr)` is a compile-time operator that returns `true` when the expression is guaranteed not to throw. `safe()` is declared `noexcept` so it yields `true`. `risky()` has no specifier so it may throw, yielding `false`. The expression `1 + 2` operates on integers with no throwing operations, so it yields `true`.

prompt_md: A function is marked `noexcept` but its body calls `std::vector::at()`, which can throw `std::out_of_range`. What happens if `at()` throws at runtime?

options.a: The exception propagates normally to the nearest catch handler.
options.b: The compiler rejects the code with an error.
options.c: `std::terminate` is called immediately, ending the program.
options.d: The exception is silently discarded and the function returns a default value.

explanation_md: The `noexcept` specifier is a runtime-enforced contract. If an exception attempts to propagate out of a `noexcept` function, the runtime calls `std::terminate` — the stack may not even unwind. The compiler does not reject the code (it cannot always detect every possible throw path), and no catch handler is invoked.

prompt_md: Why does `std::vector` prefer to move elements (rather than copy) during reallocation only when the element type's move constructor is `noexcept`?

options.a: Move constructors without `noexcept` are slower than copies.
options.b: To maintain the strong exception guarantee: if a move throws mid-reallocation, the original vector would be left in a partially modified state with no way to recover.
options.c: `noexcept` move constructors use less memory than non-noexcept ones.
options.d: The C++ standard forbids calling non-noexcept constructors inside containers.

explanation_md: The strong exception guarantee means that if an operation fails, the program state is unchanged. During reallocation, if a move throws after some elements have already been moved out of the old buffer, the original buffer is partially destroyed and the new buffer is incomplete — there is no way to undo the damage. Copying preserves the originals until all copies succeed, so a throwing copy can still roll back. Move is only safe here when it is guaranteed not to throw.

options.a: std::string copy and assignment can throw (e.g. on allocation failure), so the noexcept guarantee may be violated.
options.b: tmp should be declared as const std::string.
options.c: The function should return void instead of noexcept.
options.d: The third line should use std::move(tmp) to compile correctly.

explanation_md: Copying a `std::string` can throw `std::bad_alloc` if memory allocation fails. Marking the function `noexcept` while it performs throwing operations means that if an allocation does fail, `std::terminate` is called rather than propagating the exception. The correct implementation should use `std::move` to move-assign the strings (since `std::string` move operations are `noexcept`) or simply use `std::swap` which already has the right `noexcept` annotation.

`g()` uses conditional `noexcept`: it is `noexcept` if and only if calling `f()` is `noexcept`. Since `f()` is declared `noexcept`, `noexcept(f())` evaluates to `true`, so `g` is `noexcept(true)` which is the same as plain `noexcept`. Therefore `noexcept(g())` returns `true`.
